SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main empirical claims are supported by experiments across 43 texts from the Anselm corpus and comparisons to several baselines (Section 4 and Baselines). Training and evaluation details (optimizer, learning rate, dropout, batching, max-length filtering) are specified (Sec. 3.2, 3.6). However, the paper does not report statistical significance tests for the reported average gains (e.g. the stated 2–3% absolute improvement) and some design choices (tuning hyperparameters on a single manuscript B) introduce potential bias that is not fully quantified.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper applies well-known sequence-to-sequence and attention mechanisms and proposes multi-task learning with a grapheme-to-phoneme auxiliary task to aid historical spelling normalization (Sec. 3.5). Using G2P as an auxiliary task for normalization is a meaningful combination and analysis (claiming MTL can learn to focus attention), but the components themselves (seq2seq, attention, MTL, CELEX) are existing techniques, so the contribution is an incremental but sensible combination rather than a wholly new method.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is organized into standard sections (datasets, model, training, evaluation, analysis) and key experimental settings are described (Sec. 2–4). However, the provided text shows repetition and some editorial issues (duplicated paragraphs and minor inconsistencies), and some methodological details (e.g. exact attention variant, full architecture diagram) are only briefly described, making parts harder to follow.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Experiments are extensive across many individual texts (43), include multiple baselines (averaged perceptron, bi-LSTM tagger, Norma), and analyses such as weight comparisons, error overlap statistics, t-SNE and saliency maps (Sec. 5). The paper discusses limitations (lack of token context) and gives sufficient implementation details (Keras, hyperparameters), but it lacks statistical significance testing and would benefit from a more thorough ablation of hyperparameter sensitivity and the combined attention+MTL failure mode.

IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses the practical and relevant problem of historical spelling normalization and shows consistent gains over prior tools (e.g. Norma) and prior sequence-labeling work, which is useful for the community (Abstract, Sec. 7). While the approach and analyses may inform future MTL and attention studies, the contribution is primarily an incremental applied advance and is likely of moderate rather than transformative impact.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic (neural methods for historical text normalization and analysis of MTL vs. attention) falls squarely within NLP/ML conference scope, the methods used are standard for the venue (seq2seq, attention, LSTMs, Adam) and evaluation uses community resources (Anselm corpus, CELEX) and competitive baselines, making it a strong fit for a top NLP/ML venue.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against multiple reasonable baselines including an averaged perceptron, a bi-LSTM tagger (Bollmann & Søgaard 2016) and the Norma tool, and reports per-text evaluations across the corpus (Sec. 4). Comparisons appear fair (same dataset splits and reported averages), but the manuscript does not report statistical significance of differences and omits comparisons to any other contemporary neural normalization/character-based SMT results beyond general citations, leaving room for minor omissions.
